summary: I lived in Laos during a very turbulent time around 1975. When the Communist Party of Laos took over Long Cheng in May 1975, my family decided to leave for safety. We traveled through Laos to Vientiane, which is near the border with Thailand and the Mekong River. Laos was the place where my early childhood unfolded before we became refugees.

The Communist Party of Laos came to power in Long Cheng in May 1975, which was a turning point for my family. Their arrival created imminent danger for us, prompting my father to move us out of Long Cheng. This political change forced many families like mine to flee Laos and seek refuge elsewhere.

Long Cheng was the city in Laos where we lived before the Communist Party took over in May 1975. It was the place that became unsafe for my family, so we had to leave quickly. The events there marked the beginning of our journey toward safety and eventually to a refugee camp in Thailand.

After leaving Long Cheng, my family traveled to Vientiane, a city in Laos near the border with Thailand and the Mekong River. We were delayed at a military checkpoint on the way, where my mother gave birth to my sister Keo. Thanks to my father's kindness to a soldier there, we were allowed to pass and finally reached Vientiane before crossing into Thailand.

My family crossed the Mekong River at night to reach Thailand, seeking safety from the conflict in Laos. We arrived at Nong Khai refugee camp in Thailand, one of the first families there. Life in Thailand was difficult, with no electricity or running water, but we built shelters and gardens to survive while waiting for our next steps.

The Mekong River marked a critical boundary in our journey from Laos to safety. After reaching Vientiane, my father arranged for us to cross the Mekong River by boat during the night to enter Thailand. This river crossing was a pivotal moment that led us to the refugee camp in Nong Khai.

Nong Khai was the location of the refugee camp in Thailand where my family first settled after fleeing Laos. We were among the first refugees to arrive there, and life was very basic with bamboo shelters and no modern utilities. The camp was divided between Lao and Hmong people, and we worked hard to create gardens and raise animals to sustain ourselves.

In August 1978, my family and I arrived in the United States as refugees. We landed at the Minneapolis–Saint Paul International Airport, and I was 12 years old and did not speak any English. My cousin helped us adjust to life there by teaching us how to use everyday appliances that were completely new to us.

Our first experience in the United States was arriving at the Minneapolis–Saint Paul International Airport in August 1978. It was a big change for me since I was only 12 and had never seen things like electric ovens or microwaves before. My cousin met us there and helped us learn how to use these new things as we started our new life.
